assumptions: The declared intragroup dividend has been recognised as a monetary receivable/payable between group entities.
The analysis is for consolidated financial statements, not separate or individual financial statements.
A foreign exchange exposure exists only if the dividend receivable/payable is denominated in a currency relevant to the entities' functional currencies.
No facts are provided that the dividend-related balance is, in substance, part of a net investment in a foreign operation.

approaches.label: General intragroup hedged-item prohibition
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, only items with an external party can generally be designated as hedged items. An intragroup dividend receivable/payable is therefore not hedge-eligible under the baseline rule.
approaches.practical_implication: Absent a specific IFRS 9 exception, the recorded intragroup dividend exposure cannot be designated in consolidation.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 limits hedged items in consolidation to assets, liabilities, firm commitments or highly probable forecast transactions with parties external to the reporting entity.

approaches.label: Foreign currency hedge of an intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 creates an exception for the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item in consolidated financial statements. This works only when the related FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
conditions: The recorded dividend creates an intragroup monetary receivable/payable
The foreign currency gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
The group entities involved have different functional currencies
The hedge relationship is formally designated and documented at inception
The IFRS 9 effectiveness requirements are met
approaches.practical_implication: A booked intragroup dividend receivable can be hedge-accounted in consolidation if it qualifies as an intragroup monetary item meeting the IFRS 9 exception and hedge criteria.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to qualify as a hedged item in consolidated financial statements when the FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
Paragraph 6.4.1 requires formal designation, documentation and satisfaction of hedge effectiveness requirements.

approaches.label: Cash flow hedge of a forecast intragroup transaction
approaches.reasoning: Before the receivable is recorded, a highly probable forecast intragroup transaction may qualify in consolidation only if it is in a non-functional currency and the FX risk will affect consolidated profit or loss.
conditions: The transaction is still forecast and highly probable
It is denominated in a currency other than the functional currency of the entity entering into it
The foreign currency risk will affect consolidated profit or loss
The hedge relationship is formally designated and documented at inception
The IFRS 9 effectiveness requirements are met
approaches.practical_implication: This is an alternative only for the pre-recognition stage; once the dividend receivable is booked, the intragroup monetary item exception is the more relevant route.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits a highly probable forecast intragroup transaction to qualify in consolidated financial statements if the currency and profit-or-loss conditions are met.
Paragraph B6.3.5 states that forecast intragroup transactions that do not affect consolidated profit or loss cannot qualify as hedged items.
Paragraph 6.4.1 requires formal designation, documentation and satisfaction of hedge effectiveness requirements.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 applies only to hedges of foreign currency risk arising from net investments in foreign operations. It should not be applied by analogy to other hedge accounting relationships such as an ordinary dividend receivable.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not document the recorded intragroup dividend exposure as a net investment hedge unless the facts are actually about hedging net assets of a foreign operation.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 7 and 8 limit the Interpretation to hedges of net investments in foreign operations and prohibit analogy to other hedge accounting types.
Paragraphs 10 to 14 describe the hedged item as net assets of a foreign operation and the related foreign currency risk, not an intragroup dividend receivable.
